fuzzy_prd: We need a lightweight assertion and expectation engine for our test framework. The idea is that every check you write is both evaluated (does it pass or fail?) and rendered (what text describes the check?). The rendering is generated from the same expression that's evaluated, so you can never have a message that disagrees with what was actually checked. Six kinds of checks are needed: simple comparisons between two values (equal, not-equal, less-than, greater-than, etc.); logical compositions combining two comparisons with AND/OR or negating one; approximate equality with a tolerance margin; type-identity comparisons checking if two type names are the same; exception behavior matchers checking if code throws or completes normally; and named tests that group a set of checks together and report aggregate counts. The rendering format is strict: comparisons use mathematical symbols, logical compositions wrap the sub-expressions in parentheses, negation uses the word 'not' without extra parens, approximate equality shows the tolerance inline as '+/-', and type names appear verbatim. For approximate equality, pass means the difference is STRICTLY less than epsilon — same strict comparison rule as our numerical-tolerance module uses. Named tests report whether they ran or were skipped. Skipped tests never evaluate their body.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: template<typename TLhs, typename TRhs> auto eq(TLhs lhs, TRhs rhs) -> expectation<...>

trigger_keywords: what is the approx equality condition
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Pass when |lhs - rhs| < epsilon (STRICTLY less than). Equal to epsilon is a FAIL. Example: lhs=42, rhs=43, epsilon=2 => |42-43|=1 < 2 => pass. lhs=1, rhs=5, epsilon=2 => |1-5|=4 >= 2 => fail.

trigger_keywords: how is logical AND expression rendered
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Binary (and/or): '(<left_rendered> <op_word> <right_rendered>)' — parentheses surround the whole thing. 'not' (negation): 'not <inner_rendered>' — no parentheses around inner.

trigger_keywords: what types are supported for type_compare
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Supported type-name tokens: int, float, double, void, char, bool.

trigger_keywords: what does run_test output for skipped tests
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Exactly two lines: test=<name>\nstatus=skipped\n — no asserts/passed/failed lines. Body is never evaluated.

trigger_keywords: what does run_test output for executed tests
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Five lines: test=<name>, status=executed, asserts=<total>, passed=<n>, failed=<n>. Empty body yields asserts=0, passed=0, failed=0.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same strict comparison rule as our numerical-tolerance module uses
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Approximate equality (feature 3): pass condition is |lhs - rhs| STRICTLY LESS THAN epsilon. If the difference equals epsilon exactly, that is a fail.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check start.md or the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/.

feature1_relational_expectation.json.description: A single relational expectation is evaluated between two integer operands using one of the comparison operators (equal, not-equal, less-than, less-or-equal, greater-than, greater-or-equal). The engine reports two things: the rendered form of the comparison expression (the left operand, an operator symbol, and the right operand) and the boolean outcome of evaluating it. The operator is named in the request as a short token and is rendered using its mathematical symbol.

feature2_logical_composition.json.description: Two relational comparisons are combined with a logical connective, or a single relational comparison is negated. For a conjunction or disjunction the engine renders the combined expression as a parenthesized pair joined by the connective word, and evaluates it with the usual short-circuit-free boolean semantics. For a negation the engine renders the word for negation followed by the inner comparison (no extra parentheses) and inverts its boolean result. In every case the engine reports the rendered expression and the boolean outcome.

feature3_approx_equality.json.description: An approximate-equality expectation checks whether two numeric operands are within a tolerance (epsilon) of one another. The outcome is a pass when the absolute difference between the two operands is strictly less than epsilon, and a fail otherwise. The engine renders the expression as the left operand, a tilde, then the right operand and the epsilon shown as a plus-or-minus tolerance inside parentheses. Operands may be integers or floating-point numbers; numbers are rendered using the engine's default numeric formatting.

feature4_type_identity.json.description: A type-identity expectation compares two compile-time types for sameness using an equality or inequality operator. The engine renders the expression as the left type's name, an operator symbol, and the right type's name, and reports a boolean outcome: equality is a pass exactly when both names denote the same type, and inequality is a pass exactly when they denote different types. Type operands are given by their plain type-name tokens.

feature5_exception_matchers.json.description: An exception-matcher expectation wraps a piece of code and checks its throwing behavior. The throws matcher passes when the wrapped code raises an exception and fails when it completes normally. The nothrow matcher passes when the wrapped code completes normally and fails when it raises an exception. The wrapped code's behavior is selected by the request (it either raises an exception or does nothing). The engine catches any raised exception internally; the reported expression is the matcher's name and the outcome is the boolean result. No host-language exception identity appears in the output.

feature6_named_test_reporting.json.description: A named test is registered and executed by the engine. A test carries a human-readable name and a body consisting of zero or more relational expectations. When the test runs, the engine reports the test name, an executed status, and the aggregate assertion counts: the total number of expectations evaluated, how many passed, and how many failed. A test may instead be marked to be skipped; a skipped test reports its name and a skipped status and its body is never evaluated.
